title: Datatype conversion - why is it required

content.problemStatement:
Imagine you're a data analyst for an e-commerce company. You've been tasked with analyzing sales data to identify trends and make recommendations for inventory management and marketing strategies.

When you load the data into pandas, you notice some issues:
```python
order_id                object
date                    object
product_id              object
quantity                object
unit_price              object
total_sales             object
customer_age            object
is_returning_customer   object
dtype: object

  order_id        date product_id quantity unit_price total_sales customer_age is_returning_customer
0   OD1001  2023-01-15      P1234       2     $19.99     $39.98          35.0                  TRUE
1   OD1002  2023-01-15      P5678       1     $24.99     $24.99          28.0                 FALSE
2   OD1003  2023-01-16      P9876       3     $9.99      $29.97          42.0                  TRUE
3   OD1004  2023-01-16      P5432       1     $49.99     $49.99            -                  FALSE
4   OD1005  2023-01-17      P1234       2     $19.99     $39.98          31.0                  TRUE
```
- All columns are of type 'object' (string).
- Numeric columns (quantity, unit_price, total_sales, customer_age) are stored as strings.
- date is stored as a string, not a datetime object.
- is_returning_customer is stored as 'TRUE'/'FALSE' strings, not boolean.
- Some customer_age values are '-' instead of numbers.

The above issues will impact the following
- Incorrect Calculations - for example - you cannot sum strings to compute revenue
- Inefficient Storage - Storing numbers as strings uses more memory than storing them as integers or floats
- Date Operations - You can't perform date arithmetic or extract components (like month) easily
- Statistical Analysis - Functions like mean, median, etc., won't work on string data

In real-world data analysis, the ability to properly handle and convert data types is crucial.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
